entry.title: Missing mode selector and infinite loading when accessing a Composable Storefront in SmartEdit
entry.content:
When accessing a Composable Storefront page in SmartEdit, the mode selector (Preview, Basic Edit and Advanced Edit) is missing along with an infinite loading screen. In some cases, below errors can be observed in the browser console tab:

  • ERROR Error: Error: the page is not a valid storefront page.
  • Uncaught Error: https:/smarteditDomain is not allowed to override this storefront

This situation is caused by a misconfiguration in allowOrigins in smart-edit-feature.module.ts file. Set the SmartEdit Web Site domain in allowOrigin in the smart-edit-feature.module.ts file, as per the below example:

provideConfig(<SmartEditConfig>{
   smartEdit: {
     storefrontPreviewRoute: 'your-preview-route-value',
     allowOrigin: '<SmartEdit Web Site domain>',
   },
 })

entry.title: Comparative Analysis of Data Selection Techniques in SAP
entry.content:
In SAP systems, selecting the right data extraction method plays a pivotal role in ensuring system efficiency and performance. With vast amounts of data stored in SAP databases, it's essential to choose the right method for data retrieval, especially to keep operations responsive and well-optimized.

In this article, we will explore various SAP data selection techniques such as the Open Cursor, Core Data Services (CDS) views, Database views, and some other advanced strategies. Each has its strengths and weaknesses and understanding them will help you determine the best fit for your specific use case.

 

               1. The Open Cursor Method: A Classic Approach

The Open Cursor method in SAP ABAP is a traditional technique used for data selection. It works by opening a database cursor to fetch records in smaller, manageable sets. This approach is particularly useful when working with large datasets since it doesn't overload the system by fetching everything at once.

Syntax -

OPEN CURSOR [WITH HOLD]    cursor_name     FOR

SELECT      fields     FROM      source      

[WHERE    condition]       

[GROUP BY fields]                

[HAVING   cond]                                

[ORDER BY fields].

the WITH HOLD attribute prevents a cursor from closing when a database commit occurs in Native SQL.

the WHERE condition will help to fetch the filter data from the database

the GROUP BY will create groups based on the value of the mentioned field

HAVING will add more filtration to the data extracted

ORDER BY is used to sort the data with the fields mentioned, the default sort order is ascending, and one can use DESC to sort in descending.

Example:

 

If you use OPEN CURSOR and CALL FUNCTION 'DB_COMMIT', use the addition "WITH HOLD". The database commit won't close the cursors. NB: COMMIT WORK always closes all opened cursors.

This method ensures that only small chunks of data are fetched, which helps prevent memory overload and improves system stability when dealing with large datasets. However, it requires careful manual control of the cursor, making it less convenient compared to more modern approaches.

 

Strengths:

- Suitable for processing large datasets in small chunks.

- Prevents memory overload by limiting data fetched in each iteration.

Drawbacks:

- Requires manual cursor management.

- Lacks advanced performance optimization compared to newer techniques.

 

 

               2. Core Data Services (CDS) Views: A Modern, High-Performance Approach

CDS Views represent a modern approach to data selection in SAP, offering a more optimized and abstract way of handling data retrieval. CDS views allow you to define models at the database level using SQL-like syntax, making complex queries more manageable and improving performance. Designed to fully exploit the capabilities of the SAP HANA database, CDS views provide the ability to perform high-level operations directly on the database.

Example:

 

They can be further used to create rich CDS views ready for consumption and can primarily used with analytics tools and reporting applications like SAP Analysis for Office, Lumira, and the S/4HANA Query Browser, allowing you to expose data from your business model for analysis and visualization in various reporting formats, they are designed to be consumed by different analytical applications by providing a structured data model with specific annotations for data interpretation.

CDS views are highly efficient because they allow for powerful data manipulation at the database level, reducing the need for complex logic in the ABAP layer. They are also well-integrated with newer SAP technologies like SAP Fiori and S/4HANA.

 

Strengths:

- Optimized for performance, especially with SAP HANA.

- Declarative, SQL-like syntax makes it easier to handle complex queries.

- Provides advanced features like annotations, associations, and computed fields.

- Seamlessly integrates with modern SAP technologies.

Drawbacks:

- Requires learning and understanding of CDS concepts.

- May not be suitable for legacy systems without SAP HANA.

 

 

               3. Database Views: A Simple, Traditional Tool

Database Views in SAP provide a virtual representation of one or more database tables. While database views are not as feature-rich as CDS views, they offer a simple and effective way to combine and access related data from multiple tables. They are ideal for straightforward joins and data extraction, especially when performance is not the primary concern.

Example:

Where Database_view is the Database view created using transaction SE11.

Although database views do not provide the same level of performance optimization or flexibility as CDS views, they still serve as a useful tool in certain scenarios, particularly for simpler queries.

 

Strengths:

- Easy to define and use.

- Useful for basic data access and reporting.

Drawbacks:

- Lacks advanced capabilities like calculated fields or associations.

- Performance is lower compared to CDS views, especially for complex queries.

 

 

               4. A Comparative Overview: Choosing the Right Approach

 

The following table summarizes the key differences between the three data selection techniques discussed:

Feature

Open Cursor

CDS Views

Database Views

Performance

Medium

High

Low to Medium

Memory Usage

Low

High, but optimized for large sets

Low to Medium

Support for Complex Queries

Low

High

Low

Ease of Use

Medium

Medium to High

High

Maintainability

Medium

High

Medium

Compatibility with Modern SAP

Low

High (SAP HANA, S/4HANA, Fiori)

Low

Handling Large Datasets

High

High

Medium

 

 

               5. Advanced Data Selection Techniques and Best Practices

While the three techniques above are commonly used for data selection, several advanced strategies can further optimize performance in SAP environments:

a) Buffered Data Reads

SAP offers table buffering to speed up data retrieval for frequently accessed data. By storing table data in memory, buffering reduces the load on the database, leading to faster data access for small-to-medium-sized tables.

b) Parallel Processing

For larger datasets or more complex operations, parallel processing can distribute the workload across multiple threads. This approach can significantly reduce processing time for time-consuming operations such as joins or aggregations.

c) AMDP (ABAP Managed Database Procedures)

For scenarios requiring complex calculations or database-specific optimizations, AMDP allows you to write SQL Script (or other database-specific languages) directly in ABAP, moving the computation to the database level. This is particularly powerful for SAP HANA users, where database-side processing is much faster.

 

# Conclusion:

Each data selection technique in SAP has its advantages and ideal use cases. The Open Cursor method is a reliable choice when working with large datasets incrementally but lacks modern optimizations. CDS Views are the best choice for high performance and flexibility, especially in environments using SAP HANA or S/4HANA. Database Views offer a simpler alternative for less complex queries, though their performance and flexibility are more limited.

 

By understanding the strengths and limitations of these techniques, SAP developers can make informed decisions to optimize their applications for performance and maintainability.

Stay tuned for more insights on advanced SAP development techniques, including CDS Consumption Views in our next article!

 

"Rich CDS Consumption Views in SAP are a versatile and powerful tool for optimizing data access, especially in environments with complex data models or real-time processing needs. They are particularly beneficial for Fiori applications, real-time analytics, ABAP development, and secure data management, all while boosting performance and reducing development complexity"

 

I hope this article was helpful and added new insights to your skillset.
Thank you for reading!
Happy learning! 😊

entry.title: Connecting Cloud ALM with BPA and configuring, triggering of automation in BPA upon job failure
entry.content:
Logon to BTP cockpit

Navigate to Overview section

Take note of Tenant ID and Subaccount ID

Connecting Cloud ALM to BPA :

Log on to SAP Cloud ALM tenant

Navigate to Administration -> Landscape Management

Click on the Service

Choose Add -> New Service

Enter relevant details as below in the window that pops up

The Root URL and Logon URL are the URL's from the lobby page of BPA. Please omit /lobby before using it in the Root URL and Logon URL.

Click Save.

Scroll to ENDPOINTS

Click Add

Next step is to obtain the Service key

Logon to SAP BTP Cockpit.  

  •  Navigate to the subaccount where SAP Build Process Automation is subscribed.
  • Select Instances and Subscriptions. 
  •  Select the service instance for SAP Build Process Automation.
  •  Select tab Service Keys. 
  •  Select Actions --> Download.

Create API Key

  •  Logon to SAP Build Process Automation. 
  •  Select Control Tower.
  •  Create an API key with scopes trigger_read and trigger_execute for the environments you want to use.
  •  Copy the generated API key.

While generating the API key activate

trigger_read

trigger_execute

Activate for all environments

Save the generated Key on a notepad

Return back to CALM screen and add endpoint

Once setup, Ping connection to test.

Congratulations. You have not successfully connected Cloud ALM with BPA.

As a next step, if a job fails in CALM then it needs to trigger the automation built in BPA. Let's configure this now!

Let's create a trigger in BPA.

Log into BPA

Select project that needs to be triggered upon job failure in CALM

Create -> Process

Click on Add a trigger -> API Trigger

Create API trigger

Add a process step

Choose Automation that needs to be triggered 

Choose the automation that needs to be triggered and Save it

Most important step is to release and deploy it. Else the trigger will not be identified by CALM

Click on Editable and deploy it.

Now let's set up CALM to trigger the automation in BPA upon job failure:

Login to CALM

Navigate to Operations -> Operation Automation

Register Operational Flow -> SAP Build Process Automation

End point gets automatically identified

Process Automation section, choose the Trigger that we built in BPA

Use Case : Job & Automation Monitoring

We now need to set up the Job and Automation Monitoring.

Operations -> Job & Automation Monitoring

Choose System

Choose the job that needs to be monitored

Click on Settings -> System

Click Events -> Add

Start Operational Flow -> Add -> Add Operational Flow

Select the Configured Trigger and Save it

Congratulation, We have now set up connectivity between SAP Cloud ALM and BPA and also configured triggering automation flows in BPA upon job failure.

entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Agile Implementation using Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hi Sri,

I do not have much experience regarding SAP SuccessFactors projects. But I know SAP Cloud ALM quite well. Therefore, let me provide a more generic answer to your question:

In SAP Cloud ALM, customers can follow the SAP Activate Methodology for SAP SuccessFactors (see the read-only version in: Roadmap Viewer - SAP for Me). This methodology consists of ~300 tasks. You can use SAP Cloud ALM to manage these tasks (read the description, access accelerators, assign team members or groups of team members, document the task status, do reporting, etc.).

Configuration Definition is part of the Explore phase. Solution Configuration belongs to the Realize phase.

 

In addition, the SAP Activate methodology provides some templates (Accelerators). However, the content of documents like requirement or user stories are highly customer- and partner specific.

Best regards, Cay

entry.title: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Value is required, null given for key: ug!
EWM Warehouse Management Monitor – Enhancements with SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2
entry.content:
The Warehouse Management Monitor in EWM offers a wide range of flexibility for customizing existing monitor node content or creating own monitor content. The how-to guide “How To Add Application Content to the Warehouse Management Monitor” (see note 3004810) guides developers in how to use these options by customizing the standard monitor, adding their own application content to the standard monitor or to completely creating their own monitors / nodes etc.

However, so far the options to do simple enhancements of existing standard nodes, e.g. adding one or two additional fields like customer-defined EEW (Easy Enhancement Workbench) fields, always required* creating customer-specific nodes: It is necessary to create own copies of the existing standard nodes and the underlying source code, and to extend those with the required additional fields. Drawback: You have created Z-coding for the nodes and enhancements or corrections for the EWM standard nodes won’t automatically apply to your copies of the nodes.

*Exception: Nodes that already support “Dynamic Selections” for additional fields, e.g. the Documents nodes for Warehouse Order / Warehouse Task or the Stock and Bin -> Storage Bin node.

With OP2023 FPS2, the new feature “Monitor Screen BAdIs” for the warehouse monitor is introduced, which provides a simplified approach for extending standard nodes without requiring a copy of the standard monitor node anymore. By implementing the new monitor screen BAdI “BAdI: Custom Selection Fields in Standard Monitor Nodes” (/SCWM/EX_MON_CUSTOM_SELECT), it is possible extend the selection screens of selected standard monitor nodes with customer-defined fields, and to either integrate them into the standard selection logic, or to implement a customer-specific selection logic for the new fields that can be combined with the standard selection logic. This new feature is described in the latest version of the Monitor How-To guide, version 3.0.

Example

Example Use Case: You have extended Inbound Deliveries with a customer-specific EEW-Field “Priority” and Handling Units with a customer-specific EEW-Field “Preferred Putaway Destination”. You want to use both fields in the warehouse management monitor to be able to select e.g. Inbound Deliveries of priority 1 and 2, and to select Handling Units relevant for such deliveries that are intended to go to specific putaway destinations in your warehouse.

Prerequisite: You have already created domains and data elements for the EEW fields and extended the structures with the respective fields. You have also defined logical field names for the delivery specific EEW fields. You have implemented the necessary application logic to write and read the new fields during Inbound Delivery and Handling Unit processing.

You can use the new Monitor Screen BAdIs to enhance the Inbound Delivery selection screen with the new selection field for “Priority”, and the selection screen for the Handling Unit subnode with both the additional parent selection field “Priority” and the HU specific selection field for the “Preferred putaway destination”. The extended screens could look like this:

Figure 1 - Standard Inbound Delivery selection screen with customer specific Screen Enhancement

Figure 2 - Standard Inbound Delivery -> HU selection screen with customer specific Screen Enhancement

Steps to extend a Monitor Node with a Screen BAdI

In the latest version of the Monitor How-To Guide, you can find a detailed example of how to enhance an existing standard monitor node with customer specific fields. In addition, you can always check the example implementations for the new BAdI for guidance on how to implement each BAdI method per node. For the example use case described above, you can e.g. check  /SCWM/CL_EI_MON_CUST_SEL_INB.

Roughly summarized, the following needs to be done to enhance the standard nodes for the example Use Case described above:

  1. Creation of a BAdI implementation and implementing class, specific for your warehouse number and the technical names of the to-be-extended nodes as filters: /SCWM/WHRITEM_MON + /SCWM/WHRHEAD_INB_HU_MON.
    • Recommendation: Implement one BAdI for hierarchical monitor nodes, e.g. Inbound or Outbound Delivery Order and their respective sub nodes. This ensures that the defined selection screens and selection fields can be reused and are handled consistently for all nodes.
  2. Creation of a Z-report to define the customer specific selection screen for your EEW-fields, and to implement the screen and variant data handling. The selection screen will be included in the standard monitor node in a new subscreen area, which is located below the standard selection screens. Check the details in the Monitor How-To Guide and the comments in the example implementations for detailed information how to implement the Z-report including the sub screen.

Please note:

  • For simplicity, we will only describe the variant with EEW fields in this document, but you can use the following fields for extending the selection screens:
    1.      Standard fields for the objects that are not included in the standard selection screen
    2.      EEW-fields added to the EEW include of the relevant objects
  • If you use such fields, they can be considered in the standard selection of the monitor node. Other fields won’t be considered, as there are validity checks in place that will filter out fields that can’t be supported by the standard selection logic.
  • For the example described in this document, you can therefore use fields from /SCWM/S_WIP_Q_WHR_INBOUND (incl. EEW fields) for the Inbound Delivery selection screen, and /SCWM/S_HUHDR_MON (incl. EEW fields) for the HU selection screen.
  1. Implementation of the BAdI methods according to your use case.
    Please note: For most nodes, it is sufficient to implement the BAdI and the customer specific report to provide the mapping for the additional EEW-fields to the standard node. The standard selection logic can directly consider the additional selection values. This does not apply for HU-specific selections, though – for HU-specific nodes, an additional customer-specific pre-selection must be implemented in the BAdI. You can find details in the Monitor How-To Guide and also in the node specific example implementations.  
    • The pre-selection logic should return HU keys that are relevant based on the customer selection values. It is recommended to carefully design this pre-selection logic, as it might have an impact on the performance of the overall node execution. Please note that if the pre-selection does not return any results, the standard selection will not continue, either.
    • The maximum number of returned HU keys is 32.000, if this is exceeded the monitor selection will not continue
    • The pre-selection logic will only be called if customer specific selection fields are used. If only standard fields are used, the standard selection will be executed and the pre-selection logic will be skipped.
  2. Specifying the screen enhancements in the BADI implementation, to connect standard screen and custom subscreen area from the Z-report.

You can find the screens that should be enhanced with to custom subscreen area in the BAdI Implementation section “Screen Enhancement”

Figure 3 - Example Screen Enhancements for Inbound Delivery node examples

Conclusion

With the new Monitor Screen BAdI feature, simple selection screen enhancements of existing standard monitor nodes are now possible without the need to copy existing nodes to customer nodes. This reduces the overhead when implementing corrections or upgrading, as the standard nodes will get any corrections and enhancements, and no customer nodes need to be adapted in addition.

Monitor nodes extended by customer selection fields still behave like any other standard monitor node, e.g. you can use standard functionalities like setting up automatic refresh, creating selection variants for the node incl. the customer selection fields, scheduling background executions of the variant selections, etc., if the Screen BAdI is correctly implemented.

In which cases does it make sense to use the Screen BAdI?

  • Simple enhancements of existing nodes, where standard selections can include the fields, e.g. Inbound Delivery Items with EEW fields
  • Simple enhancements of existing nodes, where HU pre-selection can be implemented in a simple way to pre-select values based on customer fields (e.g. selecting HUs with additional standard selection fields, e.g. open WTs, or HU EEW fields).

In which cases does it not make sense?

  • Enhancements of nodes with fields that are not supported by the original node selection, e.g. because the requirement is to combine selection information of multiple objects, or the selection fields is not even a standard field (e.g. enhancing the HU document bin selection with a field for number of completed warehouse tasks)
  • Enhancements of nodes with a complex set of selection criteria, or special requirements for the selection that does not match the logic of the standard monitor node.

In theses cases, the creation of an own monitor node with a customized selection logic is recommended. The Monitor How-To Guide provides detailed examples for the creation of customer specific nodes as well.

FIND OUT MORE!

To see more details on the above mentioned topics (such as examples of configuration screens), as well as see what other enhancements we delivered with this release, I highly encourage you to explore our blog SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Extended Warehouse Management Private Edition 2023 FPS02– What’s New .

You can also find more technical details and documentation on the above listed topic and others we left out via the “What’s New” viewer on the Help Documentation portal.

For more information about SAP Extended Warehouse Management, please follow us on social media, our YouTube channel or our community pages:

SAP EWM Community

SAP YouTube Channel

EWM LinkedIn Community

SAP Extended Warehouse Management Roadmap Explorer

Release information and restrictions of embedded EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023

Release information and restrictions of Decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023

For a complete list of Q&A from the EWM community, please access this link:
https://answers.sap.com/tags/01200615320800000705

In case you do not find your specific question there, feel free to post your question via the following form:
https://answers.sap.com/questions/ask.html?primaryTagId=01200615320800000705

entry.title: How to convert SAP module pool programs and screens into SAP BTP?
entry.content:
Hello Team,

I am new to this area and have developed an application in the ECC on-premises system. I would like to deploy it onto SAP BTP. From my research, I have found that SAP BTP does not support Classic Workflow, BDC, Classical FM, classical programming, ALV Grid Controls, module pool programs/screens, Web Dynpro components, SAP Script and Smart Forms, or any custom development objects.

The application I developed in the ECC system includes some of the objects mentioned above. I am currently analyzing it using the custom code migration app from the SAP BTP ABAP Environment.

My question is, since SAP BTP does not support the objects listed above, how can we convert them for use in SAP BTP?  or how can we make that application cloud-ready? Is there a specific tool or extension available from SAP to facilitate this conversion? 

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: BTP FinOps: Keeping Track of your Credits
entry.content:
Hello @Willem_Pardaens ,

Thanks for providing the out of box open source app. That's really helpful in some aspects as compared to standard cockpit costs & usage. We have implemented it in our BTP environment.
1. About Managed tags, on what basis do we tag the % based on LOB? Does it really look into the actual usage of XYZ LOB and service usage consumption units for those users? OR as a app user we define the % with static values?

entry.title: Issue with Employee History Salary Field for Hourly Pay Component in Variable Pay
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

I’m facing an issue related to Variable Pay, and I’m wondering if anyone else has encountered the same problem.

The "Salary" field in Employee History is mapped to the sum of a Pay Component Group. This Pay Component Group has several associated pay components in EC: one with an hourly frequency. When the employee has a monthly pay component, the value is annualized correctly and pulled into the Employee History. However, when the employee has the hourly pay component, the salary shows as 0 in the Employee History.

In EC, everything looks fine, and the Pay Component Group is annualizing correctly for both the monthly and hourly pay components. However, the Employee History remains at 0.

Has anyone faced this issue before, or does anyone have any suggestions for resolving it?

entry.title: How to check which Auth Checks a program in SAP does
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am looking for a way on how to find all AUTHORITY-CHECKs for a given transaction, report or Fiori App without manually looking through the code.

I have encountered countless situations where I added a new tile for a transaction or Fiori app and then user complained about not having access to certain information because of missing authorization settings in their role. PFCG is already a powerfull tool but doesn't always catch all auth checks since it (afaik) relies on presets for the auth objects in the SAP system.

Recently we added a 3rd party software from a company to our collection and it was my duty to create all the new tiles. Since I am getting tired of "maintaining PFCG, transport role, user complains, check SU53, maintain PFCG again, transport again, rinse and repeat" I would like to ask: Is there a already implemented transaction by SAP that I can give a transaction or report name and it spits out all the auth checks so I have a list  of "all auth checks for that report"?.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Why is Hungarian Notation Harmful?
entry.content:
@rosenpetrovIMO if you have trouble distinguishing between a local variable and a class member your problem isn't solved by HN but by choosing better names in general.

Silly example but think about this:
Within a methode, the lifetime of a variable is very short, someone could say it's temporary, so why not use sales_orders for the class member variable and temp_customer_sales for the local one? Yea i know temp_ is basically a malformed HN but I hope you get the idea 😉 .

entry.title: Re: Why is Hungarian Notation Harmful?
entry.content:
Easy way out: Use "me->" if you need to make it clear.

me->sales_orders = sales_orders.

entry.title: Re: Why is Hungarian Notation Harmful?
entry.content:
@rosenpetrov 

How you distinguish between class member table sales_orders and local table sales_orders ? mt_ and lt_ gives you immediate sign - local or class member variable, so i know that i'm modifying a local variable and not the class member...

Personally I use me-> or classname=> for accessing class members.

C# uses this, VB.NET uses Me, ABAP has the me keyword

I haven't done OOP ABAP, but I have read some OOP code. Totally forgot about that 😅

entry.title: Customize with confidence on the SAP Build Extensibility Wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
In my conversations with customers, I keep hearing that extensibility is extremely important to them. It allows their systems to adapt and grow with their businesses and enables customization to fit specific needs and easy integration with other tools, enhancing overall efficiency and user experience. With SAP BTP, we are providing a stable and reliable way to keep innovating and expanding your SAP solution beyond its standard capabilities. This year at SAP TechEd, we announced the general availability of the new SAP Build Extensibility Wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
 
The Extensibility Wizard is a tool that helps you quickly and easily add new features or customize the standard capabilities of your SAP software to better fit your business needs, without requiring extensive technical expertise.

In this blog, I’ll recap what we shared at SAP TechEd 2024 around the Extensibility Wizard, its main business value for customers, and provide some key resources to help you get started. I’ll also share my thoughts about how this release fits into SAP’s vision for extensibility and reveal a sneak peek at what lies ahead.

SAP Build Extensibility Wizard at TechEd 2024

 

 

 

 

 

The Extensibility Wizard was among this year’s key announcements at SAP TechEd. During the keynote, we also shared the news that ABAP environments are now part of SAP Build, and that customers can develop ERP extensions within a single suite of AI-driven tools. The Extensibility Wizard takes this a step further by bringing the ABAP and non-ABAP worlds together directly within the ERP, making SAP Build the default solution to modernize your core system. See all of this year’s main announcements in the TechEd keynote replay: SAP TechEd 2024 Keynote - Where ideas get real.

In case you missed it, I highly recommend checking out these on-demand sessions featuring the Extensibility Wizard, including strategy, common scenarios, demos and more:

  • AD102 | Unlock SAP S/4HANA Cloud extensibility with SAP Build
  • AD109 | Experience the next level of enterprise automation with SAP
  • XP206 | Achieve enterprise automation with SAP BTP solutions and SAP Signavio
  • IN105 | Unleash the power of AI-driven enterprise automation with SAP solutions

What’s the main business value of the Extensibility Wizard?

From SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 onwards, the wizard is now available in every business application that is enabled for key user adaptation. Both key users and developers can launch the wizard directly from their SAP S/4HANA system to start creating on-stack extensions, as well as side-by-side extensions with BTP and SAP Build. In the future, we’ll plan to enable it in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud and on-prem.

See these blogs to catch up on what’s available and how to get started:

  • SAP Build Extensibility Wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Creating side-by-side process extensions from SAP S/4HANA Cloud 
  • The Extensibility Wizard: One Tool to Rule Them All?

 

 

 

So with the Extensibility Wizard readily available, what’s the business value customers can now achieve? Here are the main benefits to know:

  • All extensibility options brought together in one place: The Extensibility Wizard consolidates on-stack extensions that are already possible like custom field and logic, together with side-by-side – bringing BTP and S/4HANA Cloud together in a single extensibility space.
  • Contextualized experience for extension development: One of the wizard’s main capabilities is that it automatically understands the business context of the application you are using and filters the corresponding extension points, such as APIs and events.  For on-stack extensions, it navigates you to the respective business application or key user extensibility apps.
  • Leverages generative AI and other BTP innovations: By providing seamless navigation into SAP Build, now customers and partners can take advantage of the latest BTP capabilities, such as AI agents and prebuilt content, to truly accelerate their ERP modernization.

SAP Build as part of SAP’s overall vision for extensibility

With the release of Extensibility Wizard and the addition of ABAP environments to SAP Build, we are realizing our vision for more accessible extensibility and clean core development.

As the graphic below illustrates, SAP Build provides an entire suite of extensibility options to meet every type of developer persona or business need. From low- to pro-code, backend to frontend development – all of them are under the umbrella of SAP Build.

Just as SAP Build has made app development accessible to citizen developers, the Extensibility Wizard now makes extension creation a smoother and more streamlined experience that is clean core by default.

It guides key users through the first steps, making it easier to start and know which tools to use. Professional developers no longer need to start from scratch, as they can leverage features like gen AI and maintained, prebuilt content. SAP Build continues to enable fusion development from multiple angles, making it a truly holistic portfolio for extensibility.

Want to learn more about our vision for extensibility?

  • See five use cases of SAP Build extending SAP S/4HANA for finance, supply chain and other lines of business in the eBook.
  • Explore our overall strategy for clean core and extensibility model in this whitepaper.

Looking ahead to a new Extensibility era

With the Extensibility Wizard and our other TechEd milestones, we’re thrilled to take extensibility into a new era, where the worlds of BTP, S/4HANA, and ABAP come closer together! Developers and business users alike will be able to extend and innovate their core systems based on their specific skill sets and custom needs. And it’s only the beginning.

This visual illustrates how SAP Build encompasses the development of enterprise-ready extensions. You’ll note that generative AI is a centerpiece of the portfolio, with many new capabilities announced at TechEd to further accelerate the development process. AI is already changing how we interact with technology and develop software, and it’s a trend that will continue to shape the entire industry.

So, what’s next for the SAP Build Extensibility Wizard?

Currently, it’s possible to create custom fields, logic, and process extensions in SAP Build Process Automation. The option to create custom apps with SAP Build Code is on the roadmap.

We’re also pleased to announce that the Extensibility Wizard is targeted for SAP SuccessFactors in the future, offering a more robust and standardized way to enhance core HR systems. The Extensibility Wizard will operate with the same behavior and framework as it does in SAP S/4HANA, integrating SAP Build within Employee Center with numerous event hooks available.

For a sneak peek at how this will look, check out the announcement from this year’s SAP SuccessConnect event in Lisbon by Siva Sundaresan, SVP of Applications Engineering at SAP SuccessFactors. He also shares a demo of the Extensibility Wizard in the Onboarding application: Road Map and Vision Session (starts around 15:45).

***

Hopefully this got you inspired and ready to kick off your transformation journey with the SAP Build Extensibility Wizard. There are plenty more capabilities to come, so stay tuned!

For additional learning, see these Devtoberfest sessions and demos:

  • S/4HANA Extensions: Start Fast with the New Extensibility Wizard
  • What software developers want to know about Key User Extensibility
  • Demo - Easily extend SAP S/4HANA to create clean, core compliant extensions using the extensibility wizard
  • Demo - GenAI and SAP Build: Accelerate Development Projects

entry.title: Re: Field "Ship-to Party's Customer Reference Date" in SD...
entry.content:
Hi @ciskoy,

Unfortunately, this field VBKD-BSTDK_E is not exposed in the released CDS view, which means you are also not able to get the value of this field in custom logic. The current workaround is to create a custom field and manually enter the same date into it.

We don't have documentation for mapping the technical name. Basically, all field names follow the Global Field Name. (Also, the technical name is not user-friendly for the"non-SAPer" 😞 ).

If you need the field "Ship-to Party's Customer Reference Date" (VBKD-BSTDK_E) via standard Data Source Extension, please consider raising a new feature request (2963059 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me).

Best regards,

Troy

entry.title: Re: Why is Hungarian Notation Harmful?
entry.content:
Not using HN is only possible in combination with "Clean Code".

"Clean Code" describes the length of a method as "short" (indeed very short!). You shoud see whether a variable is local or a class attribute by simply looking at the code.

METHOD methodname.
  DATA(today) = sy-datlo.
  tomorrow = today + 1.
ENDMETHOD.

 (and we don't have to talk about global variables because you don't need to use them at all).

entry.title: IAM Information System App – your one-stop shop for identity and administrative management queries
entry.content:
Have you ever faced challenges as an Administrator trying to identify which applications a specific Business user can access, determine how many business users are linked to a particular application, or analyse which fields have 'write' restrictions for a given user? Even straightforward tasks, such as figuring out which Launchpad pages are available for a specific Business role, can become unnecessarily complex.

The IAM Information System App is the comprehensive, user-friendly solution to these challenges. This app is not only intuitive but also robust in its capabilities, streamlining access management and simplifying administrative tasks.

Before exploring the use cases, it is important to note that this application is part of business role template “SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR/BR_APPL_SUP_ENG_DEV_SUP” and accessible through the business catalog business catalogs “SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RA”, “SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM”, “SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_RM_DISP_PC”, “SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_UM”, “SAP_CORE_BC_IAM_UM_DISP_PC”, and “SAP_A4C_BC_IAM_DISP_PC”.

Now let’s explore its key use cases and discover how this powerful tool can enhance your efficiency and effectiveness as an Administrator.

Case 1: Leveraging the IAM Information System App to Identify Applications Accessible to a Business User

To view the applications accessible to a specific business user, select the “Main Entity” – “Business User”, input the desired 'Business User ID', and click 'Go'. This will display a comprehensive list of applications available to that Business user.

For instance, 889 applications are accessible to the business user CBXXXXXXXXXX.
You also have the option to download an Excel sheet that contains the complete list of applications accessible to a business user.

Similarly, you can view all other items associated with a particular business user, including Business Role, Business Catalog, Restrictions, Launchpad Space, and Launchpad Page.


                                                                            Figure 1: Case 1(a)

Figure 2: Case 1(b)

Case 2: Utilizing the IAM Information App to Identify All Users with Access to a Specific Application

To identify users associated with a specific application, select 'Application' in the main entity filter, enter the application's name in the 'Applications' filter, and click 'Go'. This will display the Business User Names and Business User IDs of all users linked to the application.

For example, searching for the 'Manage Workforce' application reveals all business users with access to it. Additionally, you can download the results in an Excel sheet, as demonstrated in the previous case.

Similarly, you can also view the relationships between this application and the Business Role, Business Catalog, and Business Role Template

                                                                           Figure 3: Case 2

Case 3: Utilizing the IAM App to Identify Restriction Fields with 'Write' Access for a Business user

To identify the restriction fields with 'Write' access for a user, select 'Restriction' in the main entity filter, 'Write' in the access category filter, and the Business User Name in the corresponding field, then click 'Go'. This will display all restriction fields where the user has write access.

For example, in the image below, we can see the restriction fields available for the user 'XXXXXX'.

Figure 4: Case 3

Case 4: Identifying the Pages Accessible to a Specific Business Role

To identify the pages accessible to a specific business user, begin by selecting the 'Launchpad Page' under the 'Main Entity.' Next, choose the relevant 'Business Role' and click 'Go.' This will display all the Launchpad pages associated with that business role. For example, in this case, the 'Production Planner' business role has four Launchpad pages available.

Figure 5: Case 4

Case 5: Evaluating the number of roles or applications associated with a specific Business catalog

To identify the Business roles and applications associated with a specific business catalog, begin by selecting ‘Business Catalog’ under the ‘Main Entity’ field. Enter the desired ‘Business Catalog ID’ and click ‘Go’. The results will display the associated business roles in the first tab and the linked applications in the third tab.
For instance, when analysing the business catalog ‘SAP_FIN_BC_APAR_DP_REQ_PC’, the system displays three business roles—Accounts Payable Accountant, Accounts Receivable Accountant, and Project Manager - Professional Services—in the first tab. Additionally, the third tab lists four associated business applications: Manage Journal Entries - Old Version (Deprecated), Manage Supplier Down Payment Requests, Manage Customer Down Payment Requests, and User Default Parameters. This streamlined process allows for efficient identification of the Business roles and applications tied to a particular Business catalog.

Figure 6: Case 5(a)

Figure 7: Case 5(b)

Case 6: Evaluating the number of roles or applications associated with a specific Business Catalog.

To identify the applications associated with a specific Business Role Template, begin by selecting "Business Role Template" under the "Main Entity" Next, input the desired Business Role Template ID and click "Go." This will display all applications linked to the specified Business Role Template. For instance, in the figure below, the Business Role Template ID “SAP_BR_INVENTORY_MANAGER” was entered, revealing 164 associated applications.

Figure 8: Case 6

There are other opportunities you can take advantage of with this application. I highly recommend giving it a try and exploring all that it has to offer.

Thank you for reading this blog post, hopefully, the blog post was informative and helped you to understand in detail how to use IAM Information System App.

entry.title: EWM Outbound Process – Consolidation different HU into one shipping HU (What is the Best Practice?)
entry.content:
Hi SAP EWM Gurus

We have the following requirements:

We pick from different areas into internal boxes (containers with a fixed HU number), which are then sent to the packing area. There the goods are packed into the shipping carton and the shipping HU is created. If there are several internal boxes for one delivery, these should be packed together so that we just send one parcel to the customer.

Our idea was to pick the goods with the RF scanner and then pack them using the Fiori app “Pack Outbound Deliveries”. With the Fiori app, however, we have the problem that we only see one HU at a time (and since we have pre-printed container HUs, we have no barcode with the delivery).

Example select with HU as Reference Number:

Example select with Delivery as Reference Number:

Now I ask myself, what is the best way to do this? Does SAP EWM have a different process in place? Of course, we could develop something, but I think I just miss something.

I am very grateful for any input or process ideas.

Best regards

Simon

entry.title: Streamlining Business Processes with B2B Integration Factory
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business world, effective communication and efficient data exchange between business partners are crucial. The B2B Integration Factory, a powerful solution designed to simplify and streamline B2B interactions, can transform your business processes. 

In one of our recent projects, I delved deep into the possibilities of the B2B Integration Factory and discovered some insights. At first, it can feel like a lot to take in. But once you get the hang of it, you'll see how useful it can be.  

What is B2B Integration Factory? 

The B2B Integration Factory is a service within SAP that enables organizations to manage their B2B processes more efficiently.

The Partner Directory offers centralized partner management, enabling you to store and manage all partner-specific configurations in one place. This ensures consistency and simplifies the maintenance of partner data. 

Additionally, the B2B Integration Factory provides a wide range of predefined integration content, including mappings, schemas, and connectivity options, which significantly reduce implementation time and effort. 

It is built to handle high transaction volumes and can effortlessly scale to meet your business's growing needs. It supports a variety of sender and receiver communication protocols, including AS2, SFTP, HTTP, and also custom options. 

Last but not least, the B2B Integration Factory ensures secure data exchange with features like encryption, digital signatures, and compliance with industry standards such as EDIFACT, X12, TRADACOMS and more 

 Conclusion 

The B2B Integration Factory in SAP makes B2B interactions easier and more efficient. It cuts down on manual work, reduces mistakes, and speeds up transactions. By using ready-made content, it saves on setup and maintenance costs. Plus, with real-time monitoring and analytics, businesses can keep track of data exchanges and stay competitive in today’s connected world. 

If you would like to learn more, please Contact Proclus - www.proclus-group.com

entry.title: reset user password through command line
entry.content:
Hello,

My SAP server works on windows and using S4/Hana DB and I'm trying to reset user's password through command line.

I've found that it is possible by using R3trans command and prepared password_reset.txt file as below;

export
client=400
user=username
password=123456

and run this command;

R3trans -w password_reset.log password_reset.txt

but it gives me this output

4 ETW000 R3trans version 6.26 (release 777 - 19.06.24 - 15:07:06).
4 ETW000 unicode enabled version
4 ETW000 ===============================================
4 ETW000 
4 ETW000 date&time   : 06.12.2024 - 13:31:45
4 ETW000 control file: password_reset.txt
4 ETW000 > export
4 ETW000 > client=400
4 ETW000 > user=SONERCTEST
4 ETW000 > password=123456
4 ETW000 R3trans was called as follows: R3trans -w password_reset.log password_reset.txt
3WETW000 obsolete R3trans option ignored: USER=SONERCTEST (line 3).
4 ETW000 
2EETW000 error when reading the R3trans controlfile
2EETW000 unknown option PASSWORD = yes at line 4, symbol "=".
2EETW000 error when reading the R3trans controlfile
2EETW000 syntax error at line 4, symbol "123456".

What am i doing wrong here?

entry.title: Connecting SAP DMS with SAP Cloud Solutions (C4C, ariba etc) to archive documents on On-Prem Archiv
entry.content:
Our goal is to connect various cloud systems to our on-premise archive via the SAP Document Management Service, integration option (DMS) (to store documents). As a first step, we want to integrate the C4C system. Unfortunately, we are having difficulty finding specific documentation for this process. The only documentation we have so far refers to the integration of an S/4 HANA cloud system, but not to C4C or other cloud services. We are looking specifically for C4C and Ariba connection. 

Is there any best practices or guides from SAP that could help the customer to realize the case? 

Any documentation and guidance helps!

entry.title: HELP! The database migration is too slow! #DMOVE2S4
entry.content:
Transitioning to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition requires data to be moved. In case of your business systems, it might be hundreds of gigabytes or even several terabytes that need to be transferred. Naturally, you want this transfer to be safe and sound – and quick.

This blog collects performance aspects of DMOVE2S4 of Software Update Manager 2.0.

 

Software Update Manager is a tool that offers you a guided and consistent migration to the cloud.

For the migration part, two options are available:

  • DMOVE2S4 – based on Database Migration Option (DMO); recommended
  • DMO with System Move

Both procedures can be combined with a system conversion to SAP S/4HANA or an upgrade of the SAP S/4HANA system.  The DMO of Software Update Manager is a well-established technology, for which you find more information in the following blogs and of course in the official DMO guide available at help.sap.com

  • What is DMO?

Blog: Database Migration Option (DMO) of SUM - Introduction

Blog: DMO: technical background

DMO Guide at help.sap.com: Introduction - Basic Concepts - The DMO Concept

  • What is DMOVE2S4?

Blog: DMOVE2S4 – your one-step approach into the cloud

DMO Guide at help.sap.com: Planning - DMO Move to SAP S/4HANA (on Hyperscaler)

  • What is DMO with System Move?

Blog: DMO with System Move - the use case to change PAS host during DMO

DMO Guide at help.sap.com: Planning - Performing DMO with System Move

 

Which approach should I choose?

When planning a move to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, DMOVE2S4 is the recommended approach. It allows downtime-optimization approaches on top (such as downtime-optimized DMO and downtime-optimized Conversion). If the recommended thresholds for network quality (see DMO guide) cannot be met, DMO with System Move is the option of choice. As all data is transferred during downtime, consider using the parallel mode. 

 

DMOVE2S4: Performance aspects of the migration

As we have seen, the safe and sound part is covered – your data is moved by a well-established, proven tool with thousands of successful executions. However, what needs to be monitored individually in every case, is the duration of the migration and the related downtime of the system.

If you are facing long migration phases, consider the following aspects:

 

1️⃣   Network quality

All your data needs to be moved from point A to B. If you network only allows to transfer 1GB/hour and you need to transfer 120GB, it is physically not possible to be faster than 120 hours, regardless of how we tweak the Software Update Manager. 

Blog: DMO = Do Math Obligations!

The Software Update Manager documents the network quality in several log files. As of Software Update Manager 2.0 SP21:

PHASE_DBSTAT.LOG / PHASE_DBSTAT.SAV ➡️latency of the connection
*RUN files ➡️speed and latency statistics of the data transfer

⚠️Especially the *RUN files are extremely useful to identify potential bottlenecks in import and / or export.



2️⃣  Optimizing the migration process with statistics in duration files

The good news is: Software Update Manager is equipped with logic to minimize that tail (i.e. lone tables increasing the overall runtime.

Blog: DMO: background on table split mechanism

It’s first calculation is sufficient most of the time – but you can improve it with feeding the actual runtimes of the previous run to the tool. For more details, please refer to:

DMO Guide at help.sap.com: Planning - Performance Optimization with Migration Duration File

 

3️⃣  Parameters

The easiest influence you have on the procedure is the number of r3load processes you configure. Please note, that more is not automatically better – refer to the blog about table splits from point 2. If you want to optimize the number of parallel r3loads, check out point 4.

 

4️⃣  Fine-tuning with Benchmarking and migration repetition

With the Benchmarking Tool of Software Update Manager, you can jump directly to the migration (without stack.xml). It also allows to select only a part of the data (e.g. largest 10% of tables) for migration. In addition, the migration repetition option of Software Update Manager allows you to rerun the downtime (and change parameters). Combining these two allows you to tweak your migration without having to rerun the whole Software Update Manager procedure.

For more information, please refer to:

Blog: DMO: introducing the benchmarking tool

Blog: Optimizing DMO Performance

DMO Guide at help.sap.com: Migration Tools - Benchmark Migration

DMO Guide at help.sap.com: Running the Software Update Manager - Special Features for DMO - Using the Migration Repetition Option for Testing

 

5️⃣  Downtime-optimized DMO

One of the easiest options to reduce the downtime is the downtime-optimized DMO. With this approach, part of the migration is done during uptime.

Blog: DMO: downtime optimization by migrating app tables during uptime

The initial selection from Software Update Manager depends on the stack. To tailor the tables that are migrated partly in uptime to your needs, Software Update Manager Toolbox allows you to select tables for uptime migration.

SAP Note: 3092738 - Software Update Manager Toolbox - Central SAP Note

⚠️Please note that rules apply to which tables can be transferred partly on uptime, such as having a primary key on database level (see SAP Note 2547309).

 

❗  For the experts: Manual intervention

If you have applied all measures above and still see a tail in execution, you might want to manually split a specific table or change the order via priority.

SAP Note: 3386079 - SUM DMO scenarios: Manual Options for Table Splitting and Table Migration

⚠️ Please note that this is not necessary in most of the cases. If you observe a fundamental miscalculation by Software Update Manager leading to a tail, feel free to contact us via ticket on component BC-UPG-TLA.

 

 

While some of the information shared here dates back to 2015, it remains highly relevant and valuable for your journey to SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The goal of this post was to bring all these insights together and paint a cohesive picture that will support your migration.

All the best!

Mirja Kempin

Software Update Manager Product Management

entry.title: Re: S/4 HANA Product Compliance, SDS Shipment goes to "Co...
entry.content:
Hi

#1: The output of safety data sheet considers the “output parameter determination” rules maintained in your system. If the execution of the rule results in output not required, all SDS shipment requests will not be output. Please check the defined rules for the application SAFETY_DATA_SHEET in the Output Parameter Determination SAP Fiori app.

#2: Most likely is the setup incomplete if output requests remain in status in preparation. You can press the link “output details” in the shipment monitor app and check the messages there. The central setup guide of Output Management is available here: Output Management Setup Guide (1LQ).

Kind regards,
Mathias

entry.title: Re: How to change bsik-xesrd.
entry.content:
This flag should be updated by standard as soon as either field BSEG-ESRNR or ESRRE would be filled with non initial value

Look for

  • FM such as FI_DOCUMENT_CHANGES or FI_ITEMS_MASS_CHANGE
  • BDC on FB02, FB09

In most cases those fields must be allowed to change (Customizing transaction OB32)

entry.title: Is there an OData API available for performing BIN Sorting via API?
entry.content:
We are unable to identify an API that would allow us to perform BIN sorting. We need this functionality as part of managing the data lifecycle for S4HPC in a side-by-side application in the BTP ABAP environment.

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Is there an OData API available for performing BIN So...
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Currently, there is no specific API available for performing BIN sorting in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud as part of managing data lifecycle for a side-by-side application in the Business Technology Platform (BTP) ABAP environment. However, as an alternative, you can consider creating custom logic in your ABAP code to perform the BIN sorting operation.

Please check the documents below for further information:

Storage Bin Sorting

Operations for Warehouse Storage Bin API   (Only available to Retrieve the Warehouse Storage Bin)

Storage Bin

 

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Improved Integration with Cloud Transport Management
entry.content:
As a customer you may need to work with more than one type of SAP BTP applications, like Integration Suite, SAP Mobile services or SAP Build apps. In such a scenario, you might have a requirement to use either a different SAP Cloud Transport Management service dedicated to a landscape for a particular SAP BTP application or choose a different transport route as you create different transport requests.

Content Agent is a tool that acts as a local agent and operates at sub account level for managing content operations for different SAP BTP services. This service acts as a technical enabler to integrate with Cloud Transport Management by providing option to transport the enterprise ready changes across SAP BTP accounts following standardised change management policies.

With the recent improvements in SAP Content Agent, it is possible to have more flexibility in integrating with Cloud Transport Management. Additionally when the transport request is created, you can also monitor the status and logs at the target node level.

Subscription to Content Agent service

To use SAP Content Agent you will need a subscription to "free" plan (saas application) which is free and auto entitled in every SAP BTP account. Along with that you will need some roles assigned to your user for which you can use pre-defined role collections. For more information, See Subscribe the UI plan and Assign User Roles and Permissions.
To enable the supported SAP BTP applications, where destination configuration is required, please refer to Supported Transport Scenarios and Supported Content Types.

Subscription to Cloud Transport Management

As a prerequisite you must have a subscription to Cloud Transport Management service and landscape configuration by creating target nodes and routes.
For more details see Subscribing to Cloud Transport Management and Configuring the Landscape.
To export content to the transport queue of Cloud Transport Management you need a destination in the source (development) SAP BTP account, see Create TransportManagementService Destination

Integration options

While launching the SAP Content Agent service, the overview page is loaded. Click on the "Cloud Transport Management" tile to see the different options and connectivity status. 

  • Destination - Select a destination from the list of available destinations targeted to Cloud Transport Management. The list will provide the destinations created in the BTP account having the prefix "TransportManagementService". By default, the destination is selected as TransportManagementService.
    For more information, see Create TransportManagementService Destination

  • Connection Status - Displays the integration status between Content Agent and Cloud Transport Management

  • Error Status - Displays the connection error details and possible workaround to restore connection between Content Agent and Cloud Transport Management.

  • Source Node - Select the source node of the transport route. You can select source node depending on the specific applications to separate transport requests based on content types.

    Note: If Source Node is not selected, the sourceSystemId property configured in the destination is used as default.

  • Go to SAP Cloud Transport Management - Launches SAP Cloud Transport Management Home screen.

View Transport Request status and logs

Once you have created the transport request using the export wizard, you can view the information of past activities performed using Content Agent UI.

entry.title: Where can I learn the basic configuration settings for delivery documents in SAP S/4HANA?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Configuring Delivery Processing in SAP S/4HANA Sales learning journey.

Overview
In this learning journey, you can learn the basic configuration settings for delivery documents in SAP S/4HANA. Both inbound and outbound processes are discussed, as well as the integration with SAP EWM.

Learning objectives
This learning journey will prepare you to:

  • Work with delivery documents
  • Maintain organizational units for delivery processing
  • Control deliveries (such as the delivery type and delivery item category determination)
  • Post a goods issue for a delivery
  • Integrate delivery processing with EWM

 

Prerequisites

  • Applying SAP S/4HANA Sales 
    or 
  • Applying Supply Chain Execution in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit
